title: I Ranked 9 Orlando Lazy Rivers by Nap-ability (I Fell Asleep in 4)
metaTitle: 9 Orlando Lazy Rivers Ranked by Nap-ability | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: One floater tested 9 Orlando resort lazy rivers for nap potential using a scientific-ish Nap-ability Index. Four naps achieved. Here are the full rankings.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: I floated nine different Orlando lazy rivers across two cheap vacation packages and scored every single one on the Nap-ability Index. Four of them put me definately, fully asleep. Slow current plus deep shade plus zero splash zones is the winning formula, and you can run this experiment yourself for about $49.
heroImageAlt: Empty inner tube drifting down a shaded resort lazy river in Orlando at golden hour
content:
The lifeguard woke me up by tapping my inner tube with one of those red rescue foam things. Apparently I had completed three full laps unconscious, which me and my wife have decided is the single greatest athletic achievement of my life. That was river number six of nine. This article is my gift to humanity.

Bottom Line Up Front: I floated 9 Orlando lazy rivers over two trips and ranked them all by how easy it is to fall asleep on them. Four achieved full naps. The best nap rivers combine slow current, heavy shade, and zero splash-zone adjacency, and Orlando vacation packages under $100 make the whole experiment shockingly affordable.

How Do You Rank a Lazy River by Nap-ability?

You use the Nap-ability Index: flotation stability × shade coverage ÷ splash-zone adjacency, scored out of 10. I did not invent this on a pool deck with a stolen golf pencil, and you cannot prove otherwise. Flotation stability measures whether the tube tips when your body goes fully limp. Shade coverage is the percentage of the loop where the sun is not personally attacking you. Splash-zone adjacency counts every dump bucket, water cannon, and unsupervised eight year old within firing range. High stability, high shade, low splash. That's the whole science.

A few disclaimers before the rankings. These are my personal opinions from my personal naps, not official reviews, and I'm keeping the resorts anonymous becuase I don't need a cease and desist from a lazy river. Also every river here is attached to a resort you can reach through an Orlando vacation deal, several of them through Westgate packages that start around $49-59.

The Rankings, From 'Why Even Float' to 'Legally Asleep'

9. The River That Is Actually a Wave Pool With Opinions — Nap Index: 1.2

Current so aggressive I did an involuntary 360 twice. There was a teenager doing cannonballs directly into the channel. Their allowed to do that, apparently. No nap occured here. No nap will ever occur here.

8. The One With the Dump Bucket on a 90-Second Timer — Nap Index: 2.0

You can hear it filling. That's the problem. Even in the far channel you're doing countdown math instead of relaxing, it's like trying to nap next to a polite volcano.

7. The Skinny One That's Really a Moat — Nap Index: 3.8

Decorative more than functional. My knees touched both walls. Nap-adjacent drowsiness achieved, actual nap prevented by constant gentle bonking.

6. The Sunny One With the Swim-Up Bar Soundtrack — Nap Index: 5.1

Great vibes, zero shade. You will fall asleep and wake up as a different, redder person. I dozed for maybe four minutes and paid for it dermatologically for nine days.

5. The Question Mark — Nap Index: 6.3

A certain lazy river shaped, I swear, exactly like a question mark. The curve of the question mark is glorious slow water. The dot of the question mark is a splash pad. You get 80 percent of a nap and then punctuation happens to you.

4. The Double-Loop With the Confused Current — Nap Index: 7.5

First official nap. The current here moves at the speed of continental drift, which is exactly right. Docked points because the two loops merge at an intersection where tubes gently pile up like the world's softest traffic accident.

3. The One Behind the Fake Volcano — Nap Index: 8.2

Nap two. The volcano provides world-class shade for about 40 percent of the loop. I woke up in the same spot I fell asleep in, which I believe means the river was napping too.

2. The Canopy Tunnel — Nap Index: 9.0

Nap three, a serious one. Real trees, fake rocks, water the temperature of mild soup. A heron watched me sleep. I respect the heron and believe the heron respected me.

1. The Long Quiet One Nobody Talks About — Nap Index: 9.6

Nap four, the famous three-lap lifeguard incident. A slow, shaded, nearly silent half-mile loop attached to a resort that clearly built it for adults who are tired. This river is the reason I keep booking 3-night packages instead of day trips. One afternoon is not enough.

Three laps. Fully asleep. A legend was born.

Can You Actually Nap on a Lazy River Without Drifting Into Trouble?

Yes — if you pick a river with high flotation stability and park yourself in the slow outer channel. The physics work in your favor: a properly limp adult in a wide tube is basically a self-righting pool toy. My data from four successful naps:

RiverNap LengthLaps Completed AsleepWoken By
Double-Loop~8 min0.5Tube pileup
Fake Volcano~12 min1Own snoring
Canopy Tunnel~20 min2Heron (emotionally)
The Quiet One~25 min3Lifeguard foam

Sunscreen first, always, and hand your phone to whoever you trust most. I watched a man baptize an iPhone on river number six and his vacation visibly ended in that moment even though four days remained.

Pro Tip: Weekday mornings before 11am are the nap window. Splash-zone adjacency drops to nearly zero because the cannonball demographic is still eating waffles. Orlando preview packages from around $49-59 usually include full resort pool access, so the lazy river costs you functionally nothing.

Why Orlando Is the Lazy River Capital of Earth

Orlando has more resort lazy rivers per square mile than anywhere I've ever been, because every resort here is locked in an arms race for your inner tube loyalty. The result is that a $49 preview package — yes, the kind with the 90-minute timeshare presentation, go in with a firm no and you're fine — gets you pool decks that would cost hundreds a night elsewhere. I've floated rivers on vacation deals that cost less than the parking at a theme park.

So that's my ranking, my methodology, and my heron. If you want to run your own Nap-ability study, browse the current Orlando deals and pick a resort with a river. Bring a towel. Set no alarms.

faqs.question: Which Orlando resorts have lazy rivers?
faqs.answer: Dozens of Orlando resorts have lazy rivers, including several Westgate properties and other resorts available through preview packages. Resort lazy rivers are typically included free with your stay, unlike water park admission.

faqs.question: Are lazy rivers free for resort guests in Orlando?
faqs.answer: Yes, at almost every Orlando resort the lazy river is included with your stay at no extra charge. That includes stays booked through discounted preview packages.

faqs.question: Can you really get an Orlando resort stay for $49?
faqs.answer: Yes. Timeshare preview packages in Orlando commonly run $49-99 for 3-4 nights at name-brand resorts. The catch is attending a 90-120 minute timeshare presentation during your stay.

faqs.question: Is it safe to fall asleep on a lazy river?
faqs.answer: Lazy rivers are shallow and monitored by lifeguards, but you should still apply sunscreen, stay hydrated, and keep valuables out of the water. Light dozing happens to nearly everyone; plan for it.

faqs.question: What is the best time of day to use a resort lazy river?
faqs.answer: Weekday mornings before 11am are quietest, with the least splashing and shortest tube lines. Late afternoon is the busiest and loudest window.

faqs.question: Do Orlando vacation packages include pool and lazy river access?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preview packages book you as a regular resort guest, so you get full access to pools, lazy rivers, and resort amenities just like anyone paying rack rate.

faqs.question: What should I bring to a resort lazy river?
faqs.answer: A towel, reef-safe sunscreen, a waterproof phone pouch if you must bring your phone, and water shoes if the deck gets hot. Tubes are provided free at nearly all resort lazy rivers.

faqs.question: Are timeshare presentations required with cheap Orlando resort deals?
faqs.answer: With preview-rate packages, yes — usually a 90-120 minute presentation, with typical requirements of being 25 or older with a household income around $50K+. You are not required to buy anything.

faqs.question: How long are resort lazy rivers usually?
faqs.answer: Most resort lazy rivers run between a quarter mile and a half mile per loop, taking roughly 10-15 minutes to complete at natural drift speed.

title: The Resort Waffle Tier List: Breakfast Buffets, Scientifically Ranked
metaTitle: The Resort Waffle Tier List: Buffets Ranked | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: A tier list of resort breakfast buffet waffles, from S-tier Belgian glory to F-tier sadness discs, scored on the rigorous Waffle Integrity Scale.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: After years of resort breakfast buffets I have developed the Waffle Integrity Scale and used it to sort every waffle experiance into tiers, S through F. Crisp-to-fluff ratio matters more than toppings, and the flip-it-yourself iron is the great equalizer of our time.
heroImageAlt: Golden Belgian waffle on a white resort plate with syrup being poured over it
content:
At a resort buffet in Tennessee I watched a man in a fishing shirt flip the communal waffle iron with the calm authority of a surgeon, and when the timer beeped the entire line went quiet. The waffle that came out was perfect. We all knew it. He knew it. He nodded at us like a man who had done this before, in another life, possibly professionally.

Bottom Line Up Front: This is a tier list of every kind of waffle you will meet at a resort breakfast buffet, ranked S through F on the Waffle Integrity Scale: (crisp exterior × interior fluff) ÷ syrup absorption failure rate. Cheap vacation deals include some shockingly elite waffle programs, and I will teach you to spot them.

What Makes a Resort Waffle S-Tier?

An S-tier resort waffle has an audibly crisp exterior, a steamy interior, deep syrup wells, and it was cooked in the last 90 seconds — that's the whole formula. The Waffle Integrity Scale weighs crispness and fluff equally, then divides by syrup absorption failure rate, which measures how fast the waffle collapses into a sad sponge. A waffle that goes limp before you find a table scores no higher than C, I don't care how pretty the grid was. Toppings are a distraction tactic and everyone knows it, if a buffet is pushing a whipped cream tower they're hiding a soggy foundation.

The Official Tier List

S Tier: The Flip-It-Yourself Belgian Station

The one with the rotating iron, the batter cups, and the timer. This is democracy's greatest achievement. YOU control the doneness. YOU accept the consequences. When you nail a 3-minute flip on one of these, the crisp-to-fluff ratio hits numbers laboratory waffles cannot touch. Found everywhere from Gatlinburg lodges to Myrtle Beach towers, and it never misses unless you personally miss.

A Tier: The Fresh Off the Line Buffet Waffle

A staff member is making these to order behind the sneeze guard and handing them over hot. Loses one tier only becuase you didn't get to do the flip, and the flip is 15 percent of the joy.

B Tier: The Mini Waffle Pile

Dozens of palm-sized waffles in a chafing dish. Individually mediocre, collectively powerful. The steam tray keeps them warm but slowly steams away their crispness, so the pile operates on a strict first-ten-minutes rule. Get there at opening or except disappointment.

C Tier: The Pre-Made Stack Under Heat Lamps

Edible. Fine. The heat lamp gives them a leathery exterior that is technically crisp the way a wallet is technically crisp. With enough butter you can negotiate with it.

D Tier: The Frozen Toaster Waffle in Disguise

You can tell. The grid pattern is too shallow and the edges are suspiciously uniform. Somebody in that kitchen is running toaster waffles through a warming oven and calling it a buffet, and honestly the audacity almost earns points back.

F Tier: The Sadness Disc

Cold in the middle, wet on the bottom, stacked so long they've fused into a single loaf. I once seperated two of these and they made a sound I still think about. No syrup can save you here. Walk away. The eggs are two feet to your left.

Respect the flip. Fear the sadness disc.

Does a Cheap Vacation Package Mean Bad Waffles?

No — waffle quality has almost nothing to do with what you paid for the room, and that's the beautiful secret. A $49-59 preview package books you into the same resort, same buffet, same rotating irons as the person who paid full rate. Me and my wife once had S-tier waffles on a package that cost less than the airport parking. The presentation was 90 minutes, the waffles were forever.

TierIntegrity ScoreCrisp Audible?Correct Response
S9.0-10Across the roomSilent nod of respect
A7.5-8.9At the tableGenuine gratitude
B6.0-7.4If you listenTake five, hedge your bets
C4.0-5.9NoButter negotiation
D2.0-3.9NoQuiet judgment
F0-1.9A wet soundEggs. Go to the eggs.
Pro Tip: The waffle station tells you everything about a buffet's soul. If the batter dispenser is clean and the iron timer works, the omelet station will also be excellent. Scout the waffle corner before committing your plate to anything.

Field Notes From the Waffle Belt

The American resort waffle belt runs roughly from Branson through Gatlinburg and down to the beaches, and buffet culture there is genuinely elite. Mountain lodges lean hard into the self-serve Belgian station. Beach resorts favor the mini waffle pile, which I assume is a logistics decision I'm not qualified to question. Out west the waffles get weirder — I met a cactus-shaped iron once and I think about him alot.

A closing note on syrup, since the comment section of my brain demands it: the little sealed syrup cups are C-tier delivery for A-tier product, the pump dispenser is efficient but joyless, and the warm carafe of syrup — rare, precious, usually hiding at the end of the line — is the single biggest tier-list multiplier in the whole breakfast room. A B-tier waffle with warm syrup plays like an A. Scout for the carafe. Tell no one where it is.

Wherever you're headed, breakfast is included or cheap at most resorts on packages under $100, which means the tier list is out there waiting for your own field research. Browse the current vacation deals, pick a resort, and go flip something with confidence.

faqs.question: Do resort breakfast buffets usually include waffle stations?
faqs.answer: Most family resorts in destinations like Orlando, Gatlinburg, and Branson include a waffle station at breakfast, often the self-serve rotating Belgian iron style. Higher-end buffets may make waffles to order instead.

faqs.question: Is breakfast included with timeshare preview packages?
faqs.answer: It varies by package. Some include breakfast or resort credits, while others don't. Check the package inclusions before booking — many all-inclusive Cancun packages cover all meals.

faqs.question: What is the best waffle at a hotel breakfast buffet?
faqs.answer: The self-serve rotating Belgian waffle iron consistently produces the best result because the waffle is made fresh and you control the cook time. Pre-made stacks under heat lamps rank lowest.

faqs.question: How do you use the rotating waffle iron at a hotel buffet?
faqs.answer: Fill the provided cup with batter, pour it evenly across the iron, close the lid, and flip the iron 180 degrees. Wait for the timer or beep, flip it back, and lift your waffle out with the provided tongs or a fork.

faqs.question: Are cheap resort packages the same resort experience as paying full price?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preview packages book you as a standard guest with the same rooms, pools, and breakfast access. The difference is you attend a 90-120 minute timeshare presentation during the stay.

faqs.question: What time do resort breakfast buffets open?
faqs.answer: Most resort buffets open between 6:30 and 7:30am. Arriving in the first 30 minutes gets you the freshest food and the shortest waffle line.

faqs.question: Why do hotel waffles taste better than homemade?
faqs.answer: Commercial waffle irons run hotter and more evenly than home models, and buffet batter is often a professional mix with more sugar, which caramelizes into a crispier exterior.

faqs.question: Which vacation destinations have the best breakfast buffets?
faqs.answer: Gatlinburg and Branson resorts are known for hearty Southern-style buffets, Myrtle Beach towers for scale, and Cancun all-inclusives for variety since all meals are covered in the rate.

title: Resort Towel Animals Ranked From 'Adorable' to 'Sleep Paralysis Demon'
metaTitle: Resort Towel Animals Ranked: Adorable to Demon | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: Every towel animal housekeeping ever left me, ranked from adorable to sleep paralysis demon on the Cuddle-to-Terror Scale. The towel monkey haunts me still.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: I have recieved dozens of towel animals across years of resort stays and ranked all of them on the Cuddle-to-Terror Scale. Swans and elephants are elite, the hanging towel monkey with sunglasses on is a war crime, and one folded creation in Cancun made me sleep with the bathroom light on.
heroImageAlt: White towel folded into a swan shape on a resort bed with flower petals around it
content:
I came back from the pool in Cancun, opened the door, and something was sitting on my bed wearing my own sunglasses. It had no face. It didn't need one. My wife said it was a towel monkey. I said we needed to change rooms. We were both right.

Bottom Line Up Front: Towel animals are ranked here on the Cuddle-to-Terror Scale: perceived softness × anatomical accuracy ÷ number of times it appears in your peripheral vision after dark. Swans win, monkeys lose, and anything with googly eyes is disqualified for reasons of international law.

Why Do Resorts Leave Towel Animals Anyway?

Towel animals started on cruise ships as a housekeeping signature and spread to beach and family resorts because guests photograph them constantly — free marketing that costs two towels and a talented human. At all-inclusive resorts in Cancun it's practically a nightly art program. Somewhere in every one of these buildings is a person who can fold a towel into a stingray in ninety seconds, and I think that person deserves a statue and possibly their own show.

The Rankings: Cuddle to Terror

1. The Swan — Cuddle Rating: 9.8

The classic. Elegant, symmetrical, impossible to be frightened by. A pair of them with necks making a heart shape is the towel-art equivalent of a standing ovation. If housekeeping leaves you a double swan, tip generously, you have been chosen.

2. The Elephant — Cuddle Rating: 9.1

Sturdy, wholesome, ears deployed at a confident angle. The trunk is load-bearing and everyone knows it. An elephant on the bed says this room is under the protection of something benevolent.

3. The Puppy With the Washcloth Tongue — Cuddle Rating: 8.4

Objectively adorable, docked points because the pink washcloth tongue means someone sacrificed a washcloth, and now their's one less washcloth for my face.

4. The Stingray — Cuddle Rating: 7.0

Rare, flat, majestic. Found it on the bed like a chalk outline of a kite. Respected it deeply. Was slightly afraid to move it, slept on the couch cushionless side of the bed instead.

5. The Turtle — Cuddle Rating: 6.5

Charming but structurally ambitious, the shell is a folded bath towel dome that collapses if you breathe near it, and once it collapses your just looking at a pile of towels with a head.

6. The Unidentifiable Blob With a Bow — Cuddle Rating: 5.0

Every resort has one folder who is still learning and I support them. Is it a rabbit? A bear? A loaf? The bow says it's festive, the shape says nothing at all. Neutral entity. Neither cuddle nor terror. It simply is.

7. The Cobra — Terror Rating: 6.8

A towel coiled into a striking position on your pillow is a CHOICE. It has posture. It has intent. Beautifully executed and absolutely not what I want at eye level when I lean down to check if my phone charger reaches the nightstand.

8. The Faceless Sitting Person — Terror Rating: 8.9

Some folders make a seated humanoid figure holding the TV remote. I need everyone to understand that this is not decoration, this is a summoning. It sat there. It held the remote. It had claimed the remote.

9. The Hanging Monkey Wearing Your Sunglasses — Terror Rating: 9.7

The apex predator of towel art. Suspended from the curtain rod by its arms, wearing sunglasses it took from your bag, greeting you at silhouette height when you open the door at dusk. My cardiologist knows this monkey by name. Housekeeping definately knew what they were doing, and I both salute and fear them.

It was wearing MY sunglasses.

What Should You Do When You Get a Towel Animal?

Photograph it, enjoy it, and when you're done, unfold it gently and hang the towels to reuse — housekeeping genuinely appreciates not having to swap out perfectly clean sculpture towels. Here's my field data on the emotional lifecycle:

AnimalFirst ReactionReaction After DarkVerdict
SwanDelightStill delightAdorable
ElephantWarmthGuardian energyAdorable
CobraImpressedUneaseBorderline
Sitting figureNervous laughLights onDemon
Sunglasses monkeyAudible yelpNew room requestedFinal boss
Pro Tip: Daily towel animals are most common at all-inclusive resorts, where housekeeping visits daily by default. Cancun all-inclusive preview packages run $149-479 and the towel-art program alone is worth the price of admission. Leave a small tip and a note; folders have been known to escalate their art for an appreciative audience.

The Towel Animal Economy

Here's the thing that gets me: this entire art form is free. Nobody charges for the swan. It's just a thing skilled people do to make a room feel like somebody cared, and it works on me every single time, even the monkey, especially the monkey. It's one of those small resort touches — like the cold towel at check-in — that turns a cheap package into a real vacation. And there's an arms race quietly happening in the folding world, too: I've started seeing accessories. Sunglasses, obviously. Little paper hats. One turtle wore a hibiscus flower like it was headed somewhere important. The day a towel animal hands me a tiny folded towel animal of its own, I will simply hand over my wallet and wish them both well. You can find that experience across all our destinations, and honestly the folding talent per capita is highest at beach resorts.

If you want to build your own Cuddle-to-Terror dataset, browse the current vacation deals and go meet your monkey. Keep your sunglasses in the safe.

faqs.question: Why do resorts put towel animals in your room?
faqs.answer: Towel animals are a housekeeping tradition that started on cruise ships and spread to resorts. They signal a serviced room, delight guests, and generate free social media exposure for the resort.

faqs.question: Do all-inclusive resorts do towel animals every day?
faqs.answer: Many do, especially in Cancun and the Caribbean, because housekeeping visits daily. The animal usually changes each day and often escalates in complexity through your stay.

faqs.question: Should you tip for towel animals?
faqs.answer: Tipping housekeeping $2-5 per day is customary at most resorts regardless of towel art, and appreciative tippers often report increasingly elaborate creations as the week goes on.

faqs.question: Are you supposed to use the towels from towel animals?
faqs.answer: Yes, they're your clean room towels, just folded. Unfold and use them normally, or hang them up if you want to reuse them and save housekeeping a swap.

faqs.question: How do you make a towel swan?
faqs.answer: Roll both long edges of a bath towel toward the center, fold the rolled towel into a Z shape to form the neck, and shape the head by folding the tip. It takes most people a few tries to get the neck to stand.

faqs.question: What is the most common towel animal at resorts?
faqs.answer: The swan is the most common, followed by elephants, dogs, and monkeys. Hanging monkeys suspended from curtain rods are a signature at beach and all-inclusive resorts.

faqs.question: How much do Cancun all-inclusive preview packages cost?
faqs.answer: Cancun all-inclusive timeshare preview packages typically run $149-479 for 3-5 nights including meals and drinks, in exchange for attending a 90-120 minute presentation.

faqs.question: Do budget vacation packages include daily housekeeping?
faqs.answer: Yes, preview packages book you as a standard resort guest, so you receive the same daily housekeeping service — towel animals included where the resort does them.

title: A Complete Field Guide to Resort Dads (You Are Definitely One of These)
metaTitle: The Complete Field Guide to Resort Dads | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: Nine species of Resort Dad, catalogued with habitat, call, and identifying gear, ranked by Dad Density Index. You are one of these. Science says so.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: After years of resort fieldwork I have catalogued the nine species of Resort Dad, ranked by Dad Density Index: gear carried × opinions offered ÷ actual relaxation achieved. Every dad at every pool is one of these, including me, including you, and it's a wierd honor to be counted.
heroImageAlt: Dad in a bucket hat and polarized sunglasses standing guard at the edge of a resort pool
content:
At a pool in Myrtle Beach I watched a man spend forty minutes establishing a base camp — two umbrellas, a cooler, a wagon, a small flag. Then he stood at the edge of it all, hands on hips, surveying the water like a park ranger. I looked down and realized I was also standing with my hands on my hips. We nodded at each other. Brothers in the order.

Bottom Line Up Front: There are exactly nine species of Resort Dad, and this field guide catalogues each one by habitat, call, and identifying gear, ranked by Dad Density Index (gear carried × opinions offered ÷ actual relaxation achieved). You are one of these. Acceptance is the first step, and a cheap vacation deal is the second.

What Exactly Is a Resort Dad?

A Resort Dad is any father who arrives at a resort and immediately assigns himself a job nobody gave him — grill inspector, towel logistics, current-strength assessor. It's not about age or even having kids present. It's a state of being that activates the moment the minivan door slides open. The species below are listed from highest Dad Density to lowest, and yes, hybrids exist. I myself am a 3-7 hybrid with seasonal 1 tendencies, my wife has the documentation to prove it.

The Nine Species

1. The Perimeter Guard — Density Index: 9.8

Habitat: pool edge, standing. Call: a slow exhale through the nose. He does not swim. He watches the water like it owes him money. Gear: polarized sunglasses, a whistle he has never once used but should of left at home. Maximum dad energy, zero relaxation, that's the ratio that makes him king.

2. The Base Camp Engineer — Density Index: 9.3

Arrives at 7:40am to claim territory with towels, clamps, and a wagon that converts into other, more specialized wagons. His umbrella angle calculations account for solar drift. By 10am he has built a structure visible from the lazy river and he will defend it untill checkout.

3. The Grill Sommelier — Density Index: 8.7

Found hovering at the resort BBQ area offering flame consultations nobody requested. Call: 'she's not ready yet.' He speaks of the communal grill like a vintage. He brought his own tongs ON VACATION, and their in a holster.

4. The Amenity Auditor — Density Index: 8.1

Walks the entire property on day one 'to get the lay of the land.' Reports back with a full assessment: gym adequate, hot tub temperature acceptable, second pool underrated. Books resorts off 3-night packages specifically so the audit-to-vacation ratio stays efficient.

5. The Waterslide Marshal — Density Index: 7.6

Stationed at the slide splash pool, catching children — his own and, gradually, everyone's. By day three the lifeguards consider him staff. Goes down the slide exactly once per trip, to 'test it,' and emerges saying woo quietly.

6. The Thermometer — Density Index: 6.9

Enters all water one inch at a time while making announcements about the temperature to a family that has already fully submerged. The process takes eleven minutes. He will describe the water as 'refreshing once your in.'

7. The Deal Historian — Density Index: 6.2

Cannot experience any amenity without calculating its value against what he paid. 'You know what this room goes for? And we got the whole package for $59.' He is insufferable and he is CORRECT, and frankly this whole website is his natural habitat — the man lives on deals under $100.

8. The Napping Decoy — Density Index: 4.8

Claims a shaded lounger 'to watch the bags.' Asleep in nine minutes, embarassing snore at eleven, sunglasses maintaining the illusion of vigilance. Lowest opinion output of any species, which hurts his Index despite elite gear.

9. The Vacation Convert — Density Index: 3.1

Rarest species. Somewhere around day two he simply... lets go. Floats the lazy river with no tube. Orders the drink with the fruit on it. His family watches in awe and mild concern. He returns home changed and spends October trying to get back, browsing Myrtle Beach deals at 11pm like it's a rescue mission.

Hands on hips. Surveying the water. Home.

Which Resort Dad Are You?

You can identify your species fastest by checking what's in your hands twenty minutes after arriving at the pool — tongs, phone calculator, umbrella crank, or nothing at all tells the whole story. Quick reference:

SpeciesIdentifying GearCallRelaxation Achieved
Perimeter GuardPolarized shadesNose exhale0%
Base Camp EngineerConvertible wagon'We're over here!'12%
Grill SommelierPersonal tongs'She's not ready'34%
Deal HistorianPhone calculator'You know what this costs?'55%
Vacation ConvertNone. Free.Contented sigh100%
Pro Tip: The Deal Historian is objectively the most useful species to travel with. Timeshare preview packages in Orlando run $49-97 for multi-night resort stays, and a Historian will have cross-referenced all of them before breakfast. Befriend one, or become one.

Can a Resort Dad Actually Relax?

Yes — the data shows most dads convert at least partially by day three, once the base camp is stable and the perimeter is secure. That's the real argument for packages longer than a weekend: species 1 through 6 need roughly 48 hours of active duty before the shoulders drop. Me and my kids have watched it happen in real time at an Orlando resort, it's like watching ice melt, slow and then all at once and then he's in the wave pool doing handstands.

So find your species, pack your tongs, and go establish a perimeter somewhere warm. The current vacation deals are full of pools that need watching.

faqs.question: What is a resort dad?
faqs.answer: A resort dad is any father on vacation who self-assigns duties nobody requested — guarding the pool perimeter, managing towels, or supervising the communal grill — instead of relaxing. It's a affectionate archetype, not an insult.

faqs.question: Why do dads stand at the edge of the pool instead of swimming?
faqs.answer: It's usually a mix of habitual vigilance and genuine contentment. Watching the family have fun while maintaining a sight line is, for many dads, the relaxed state.

faqs.question: How early should you claim pool chairs at a resort?
faqs.answer: At busy resorts, prime shaded loungers go between 7:30 and 9am. Many resorts prohibit unattended towel-claiming for hours, so check the posted pool rules before building a base camp.

faqs.question: Are resort BBQ grills free for guests to use?
faqs.answer: At most family and timeshare-style resorts, yes — communal grills are free on a first-come basis. Bring your own utensils, or befriend the guest who brought his own tongs.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest way for a family to stay at a big resort?
faqs.answer: Timeshare preview packages are typically the cheapest route: $49-99 for 3-4 nights at brand-name family resorts in exchange for attending a 90-120 minute presentation.

faqs.question: How long does it take to actually relax on vacation?
faqs.answer: Studies and common experience both suggest it takes about two to three days to fully unwind, which is a good argument for 4-night stays over quick weekend trips.

faqs.question: What should dads pack for a resort pool day?
faqs.answer: Reef-safe sunscreen, a bucket hat, polarized sunglasses, a waterproof phone pouch, and restraint. The resort provides towels, and the grill has been fine without your personal tongs since 2009.

faqs.question: Do resorts have activities for dads besides the pool?
faqs.answer: Most large resorts offer golf, fishing, pickleball, gyms, and poolside sports tournaments. Amenity Auditor types should grab the weekly activity calendar at check-in.

title: The 7 Timeshare Salesperson Personalities: A Field Guide
metaDescription: A field guide to the 7 timeshare salesperson personalities you'll meet at a 90-minute presentation, ranked by Charm Rating, plus how to say no to each.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: Sit through enough 90-minute presentations and you'll meet all seven timeshare salesperson personalities, from The Best Friend to The Closer Who Appears From Nowhere. This guide ranks them by Charm Offensive Rating and gives you the counter-move for each, becuase the $49 resort stay is worth it if you can hold your no.
heroImageAlt: Conference room at a resort with coffee cups, brochures, and a whiteboard showing vacation ownership math
content:
Forty minutes into a presentation in Vegas, my salesperson stopped mid-pitch, looked at the ceiling, and said 'you know what, forget the numbers, let me show you the pool again.' We had already seen the pool twice. That's when I understood I wasn't in a sales meeting, I was in a play, and everyone had a role, and mine was to keep saying no thank you while eating their surprisingly good danish.

Bottom Line Up Front: Every timeshare presentation is staffed from the same seven-personality roster, ranked here by Charm Offensive Rating: warmth × persistence ÷ respect for the word no. Know the roster, keep your no polite and boring, and the $49-249 preview package math works out heavily in your favor.

Why Do Timeshare Presentations Even Exist?

Presentations exist because resorts will genuinely trade you a deeply discounted stay — think $49-59 in Orlando or $249 for a 3-night Vegas package that retails around $915 — for 90-120 minutes of your vacation and a chance to pitch you ownership. That's the whole deal, and it's a fair one if you go in knowing two things: the presentation is real, and the personalities running it are drawn from the seven archetypes below. These are personal observations from alot of hours in beige conference rooms, your mileage may very.

The Seven Personalities

1. The Best Friend — Charm Rating: 9.5

You've known him for six minutes and he's already asked about your kids by name, twice. Genuinely likeable, which is the entire strategy. By minute thirty you'll feel wierd disappointing him. Counter-move: stay friendly, stay boring. 'No thank you, we're just here for the package' delivered warmly does not damage the friendship, becuase there is no friendship.

2. The Math Wizard — Charm Rating: 8.8

Arrives with a legal pad and a marker that squeaks. Adds up 30 years of hypothetical hotel inflation untill vacations look like a mortgage crisis only he can solve. The numbers move fast and always downhill in his favor. Counter-move: never argue the math, that's his home field. Just repeat that your not making a financial decision today. Repeat it exactly. Word for word. The Wizard cannot factor a broken record.

3. The Storyteller — Charm Rating: 8.0

Owns three weeks himself, or so the story goes, and has a photo album of his family at the resort in Cabo. The album may be real! The stories are lovely. Ninety minutes evaporates and you realize no numbers were ever discussed — that's the Closer's job, and the Storyteller was just softening the soil.

4. The Straight Shooter — Charm Rating: 7.2

My personal favorite. 'Look, I get it, you're here for the cheap trip. Sit through my thing, I'll sign your form, everybody wins.' Rare, refreshing, and half the time it's a persona designed to lower your guard before the tour. The other half he genuinely wants lunch. You will never know which you got.

5. The Discount Waterfall — Charm Rating: 6.4

Every no triggers a new manager approval and a price you were 'never supposed to see.' The package falls from $40,000 to $19,000 to a 'sampler' at $4,000 in under an hour, which raises questions about the first number that nobody in the room will address. Counter-move: if the price can fall that fast, it can keep falling without you. Hold.

6. The Guilt Curator — Charm Rating: 5.1

Sighs. Mentions his commission structure unprompted. Wonders aloud if you 'were ever really serious about your family's memories.' This is the personality that turns reviews sour, and reputable brands have been coaching it out of their rooms for years. Counter-move: the sigh is not your responsibility, me and my wife survived three of these and our memories are fine.

7. The Closer Who Appears From Nowhere — Charm Rating: 9.9, Menace Rating: also 9.9

You said no, the paperwork is out, and then a new person materializes beside the table like a card trick. Better suit. Calmer voice. One final question: 'what would it take?' The Closer is the boss level and the answer that beats them is beautiful in it's simplicity: 'nothing today.' Say it, smile, collect your gift certificate at the desk.

'What would it take?' — 'Nothing today.'

Is the Cheap Package Worth the Presentation?

For most people, yes — trading 90-120 minutes for hundreds of dollars in savings is one of travel's best hourly rates, as long as you can deliver a calm no on repeat. Run the numbers:

PackageTypical PriceRetail ValueEffective Hourly Rate for Your No
Orlando 4-day$49-59$400+~$200/hr
Vegas 3-night$249~$915~$333/hr
Gatlinburg 3-night$49$350+~$150/hr
Cancun all-inclusive$149-479$1,200+~$400/hr
Pro Tip: Check eligibility before you book: most presentations require attendees to be 25+, with household income around $50K+, and couples must attend together. Show up on time and stay the full length — leaving early can void the promotional rate and get you charged the full price.

How Do You Say No Without Being Rude?

Pick one sentence — 'this isn't in our budget and we don't make same-day decisions' — and repeat it without variation to every personality on the roster. Variation is what they work with; a broken record gives the Wizard no numbers, the Curator no guilt, and the Closer no crack. The staff have seen ten thousand nos, yours will not hurt them, and the whole exchange usually ends with genuine smiles and a breakfast voucher.

Armed with the roster? Good. Browse deals under $100, the Westgate specials, or the Vegas packages, and go collect your discount like the polite, immovable object you were born to be.

faqs.question: How long do timeshare presentations actually last?
faqs.answer: The stated requirement is usually 90-120 minutes. Plan for the full window plus a property tour; leaving before your required time can void your promotional rate.

faqs.question: Do you have to buy anything at a timeshare presentation?
faqs.answer: No. Attendance is the only requirement for preview packages. You can decline every offer and still keep your discounted stay and any promised gifts.

faqs.question: What are the requirements for timeshare preview packages?
faqs.answer: Typical requirements are being 25 or older, a household income around $50,000+, and married or cohabiting couples attending together. Requirements vary by brand, so read the terms before booking.

faqs.question: How do I say no at a timeshare presentation?
faqs.answer: Use one polite, unchanging sentence such as 'this isn't in our budget and we don't make same-day decisions,' and repeat it to every person who asks. Consistency ends the pitch faster than debate.

faqs.question: Are timeshare preview packages actually a good deal?
faqs.answer: Usually yes, if you can comfortably decline the sales pitch. Packages commonly deliver $400-1,200 in resort value for $49-479, which works out to an excellent trade for two hours.

faqs.question: Will the timeshare price really drop if I keep saying no?
faqs.answer: Prices frequently fall during presentations, sometimes dramatically, which is a good reason to be skeptical of the opening number. A deal that only exists 'today' is a pressure tactic, not a deadline.

faqs.question: What happens if I leave a timeshare presentation early?
faqs.answer: Leaving before the required minimum time usually voids your promotional package rate, and the resort may charge you the full retail price of the stay. Stay the full window.

faqs.question: Can single people book timeshare preview deals?
faqs.answer: Some brands allow singles, but many require couples to attend together. Check the specific package terms — singles-friendly offers exist, especially in Las Vegas.

faqs.question: Do timeshare presentations happen on the first day of your stay?
faqs.answer: The resort schedules your presentation at booking or check-in, often on day two. You can usually request a morning slot to keep the rest of your vacation uninterrupted.

title: Resort Room Coffee Makers: An Unhinged Investigation
metaTitle: Resort Room Coffee Makers: An Investigation | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: Six resort room coffee makers, one investigator, zero sanity left. Every in-room brewer ranked on the Desperation Brew Scale, from 4-cup hero to pod chaos.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: I have brewed morning coffee on every species of resort room coffee maker and ranked all six on the Desperation Brew Scale. The humble 4-cup drip machine is the unsung hero of American travel, the single-serve pod thing that lives in the bathroom is a cry for help, and one machine in Gatlinburg made a noise I can only describe as tempermental.
heroImageAlt: Tiny hotel room coffee maker brewing into a paper cup on a bathroom counter at dawn
content:
Day one of every vacation, 6:15am, I am standing in a dark kitchenette holding a paper-wrapped coffee pod, squinting at a machine I've never met, trying to figure out which flap opens. My family sleeps. The machine and I regard each other. One of us will win, and it has never once been me on the first try.

Bottom Line Up Front: This is a full investigation of all six resort room coffee maker types, ranked on the Desperation Brew Scale: cup quality × speed ÷ number of confusing flaps. Villa-style resort rooms with real kitchens win everything, which is one more quiet argument for booking timeshare-style resorts on cheap packages instead of standard hotel rooms.

Why Do Hotel Room Coffee Makers Matter So Much?

Because the first coffee of a vacation morning sets the entire day, and at 6am the lobby coffee shop is closed, the restaraunt opens at 7, and you are ALONE with whatever machine the room gives you. This is when the machine's true character is revealed. I have investigated six species across years of resort stays and scored each below. My methodology was rigorous: I drank everything.

The Rankings

1. The Full 12-Cup With a Real Kitchen Around It — Brew Scale: 9.7

Found in villa-style resort suites — the kind you get on timeshare preview packages — sitting on an actual counter next to an actual fridge with actual grounds you bought at the grocery store like a person. This is not a hotel coffee experience, it's a home coffee experience that happens to have a waterslide outside. Nothing else on this list competes. It's why me and my wife stopped booking regular hotel rooms entirely, a $49-97 Orlando package gets a full kitchen and the 12-cup lives there.

2. The Honest 4-Cup Drip — Brew Scale: 8.3

The workhorse. One button. One light. A little glass carafe like a science beaker. It makes four small cups of completely acceptable coffee in six minutes with zero drama, and I love it the way you love a dependable used truck. Its flaps number exactly one and the flap is obvious.

3. The Pod Machine, Name-Brand Edition — Brew Scale: 6.9

Fast and consistent, held back by pod scarcity: the room gives you two regular, one decaf, and expects that to cover two adults for four days. Day two becomes a rationing negotiation, day three someone is drinking the decaf and lying about being fine, and by day four your calling the front desk for pods like it's room service for survival.

4. The Pod Machine, Mystery-Brand Edition — Brew Scale: 4.6

Takes pods sold nowhere on Earth. Three flaps, no instructions, a water reservoir that detaches for reasons known only to its designer. In Gatlinburg one of these shuddered, hissed, and produced four ounces of something both weak AND burnt, which should not be chemically possible, I was dissapointed and impressed in equal measure.

5. The One-Cup Bathroom Brewer — Brew Scale: 3.2

Lives on the bathroom counter six inches from the sink, brews directly into a paper cup, and the whole room smells like coffee-adjacent steam for an hour. Everything about it whispers compromise. The coffee tastes like the concept of coffee described over a bad phone connection.

6. The Kettle and a Sachet of Regret — Brew Scale: 1.5

Some rooms provide a kettle and a single sachet of instant. That's not a coffee program, that's a condolence card. I've drunk it. I'll drink it again. But we should all be honest about what occured in that cup.

A word on the creamer situation, which deserves its own investigation someday: the shelf-stable thimbles of non-dairy something multiply overnight in room drawers like coins in a video game, and no matter how many you use, four remain at checkout. Where do they come from? Where do they go? I've stopped asking. I pocket two for the road like everyone else and I let the mystery live.

Weak AND burnt. That shouldn't be possible.

What's the Best Coffee Strategy for a Resort Stay?

Book a room with a kitchen, buy real grounds on day one, and you'll outbrew every machine on this list for about four dollars total. That's the entire strategy, and villa-style resorts make it trivial:

StrategyCost for 4 DaysCup Quality6am Viability
Grocery grounds + kitchen 12-cup~$4ExcellentPerfect
Ration room podsFree, then tearsFine, then decafDays 1-2 only
Coffee shop runs$60-90GreatNot before 7am
Instant sachetFreeRegretTechnically
Pro Tip: Front desks almost always have extra coffee pods and will send them up free — most guests simply never ask. Call before you're desperate, not after. And if your room has a mystery-brand machine, run one water-only cycle first; you don't want to know why, you just want to have done it.

The Kitchen Room Is the Whole Ballgame

The deeper finding of this investigation is that in-room coffee quality is really a proxy for room type, and room type is where budget travelers quietly win big. Timeshare-style resorts — the ones behind all the brands we track — build villas with full kitchens as their standard product, and preview packages put those villas in the $49-249 range in places like Gatlinburg. Same trip, real kitchen, real coffee, real mugs that aren't paper.

So that's the investigation. Six machines, one clear winner, several lingering questions about flap design. Browse the current vacation deals, filter for the villa-style resorts, and may your first morning cup be a 12-cup kitchen brew with the balcony door open.

faqs.question: Do resort rooms have coffee makers?
faqs.answer: Almost all US resort rooms include some type of coffee maker, ranging from single-serve pod machines to full drip brewers. Villa-style resort suites typically have full kitchens with 12-cup machines.

faqs.question: Can you ask the front desk for more coffee pods?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it's almost always free. Front desks keep extra pods and coffee kits on hand; most guests just never ask. Housekeeping can also restock daily on request.

faqs.question: Which resorts have rooms with full kitchens?
faqs.answer: Timeshare-style resorts — Westgate, Wyndham, Hilton Grand Vacations, and similar brands — build villa suites with full kitchens as their standard room type, and these are bookable through preview packages.

faqs.question: Is it worth bringing your own coffee on vacation?
faqs.answer: If your room has a kitchen or a drip brewer, yes — a small bag of grounds costs about $4 and outperforms room pods all week. For pod-only rooms, bring compatible pods if you can identify the machine type.

faqs.question: Why does hotel room coffee taste bad?
faqs.answer: Small single-serve machines run water too fast and not hot enough for proper extraction, and pre-portioned pods are usually stale. Larger drip machines with fresh grounds fix both problems.

faqs.question: Should you run a cleaning cycle on a hotel coffee maker?
faqs.answer: Running one water-only brew cycle before your first cup is a reasonable habit. It rinses the internal lines and takes about two minutes.

faqs.question: Do timeshare preview package rooms include the same amenities as regular bookings?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preview guests get standard rooms or villas with all normal amenities, including kitchens where the resort has them. The only added obligation is the sales presentation.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest way to get a resort room with a kitchen?
faqs.answer: Timeshare preview packages are typically the cheapest route to villa-style rooms with full kitchens — commonly $49-99 in drive-to destinations like Orlando and Gatlinburg.

title: Which Vacation Town Would Survive a Zombie Apocalypse? A Deeply Serious Ranking
metaTitle: Vacation Towns Ranked for the Zombie Apocalypse | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: Eight American vacation towns ranked by zombie apocalypse survivability, scored on defensibility, food reserves, and escape routes. Gatlinburg fans, good news.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: I ranked eight major vacation towns by Survivability Quotient: defensibility × food reserves × escape routes ÷ tourist density. Mountain towns with one road in are fortresses, flat towns with infinite parking are buffets, and Williamsburg has a seperate advantage nobody is talking about: thousands of people who already know how to churn butter.
heroImageAlt: Foggy mountain road leading into a vacation town at dusk with motel signs glowing
content:
Somewhere around hour six of a road trip, my brother-in-law asked the question that has consumed my family for three years: if it all went down, which vacation town do we drive to? We have argued about this at four different resorts. A waiter in Branson contributed a map on a napkin. This article is the definitive settlement of that argument, and yes, I'm prepared to defend it.

Bottom Line Up Front: Eight vacation towns, ranked by the Survivability Quotient: defensibility × food reserves × escape routes ÷ tourist density. Mountain chokepoint towns dominate, beach towns struggle, and the winner is a place you can currently visit for $49 while civilization remains fully operational.

How Do You Score a Town for the Apocalypse?

You score it on four factors: defensibility (natural barriers, chokepoints), food reserves (buffets per capita is a real metric here), escape routes (roads, water, ridgelines), and tourist density (every visitor is a future problem, statistically speaking). I weighted buffets heavily becuase canned goods run out but a town built to feed 40,000 people a day has warehouses, walk-ins, and grease you can trade. This is deeply serious research conducted entirely on vacation, which is the only correct venue for it.

The Rankings, Least to Most Survivable

8. Orlando — Survivability Quotient: 2.1

I love Orlando, I'm there twice a year, but let's be honest: it's flat, it's sprawling, there's a hundred thousand hotel rooms and every one of them is a door you have to check. The highway network that makes it a perfect $49-59 vacation town becomes a delivery system for trouble. Counterpoint: infinite pool water and enough turkey legs in cold storage to last a decade. Still. The density math is brutal, their just too many of us there.

7. Myrtle Beach — Survivability Quotient: 3.4

Sixty miles of open beach sounds like an escape route untill you realize it's also sixty miles of unguardable border. The condo towers of Myrtle Beach are decent vertical fortresses — knock out one stairwell and a 20th-floor unit is very defensible — but resupply means going DOWN, and down is where the problems are. Points awarded for the shrimp reserves and a truly heroic number of pancake houses.

6. Cancun — Survivability Quotient: 4.8

The all-inclusive resort as survival compound: walls, gates, generators, months of food, a swim-up bar that converts to a water reserve. Genuinely strong. The problem is the hotel zone sits on a narrow strip with limited exits, so your either committed to the compound life or you're not. Should of ranked higher purely on food logistics — those buffets feed thousands daily — but the geography is a one-way door.

5. Las Vegas — Survivability Quotient: 5.5

Hear me out: casinos are windowless bunkers with industrial kitchens, security infrastructure, and enough canned goods below the Strip to feed a small nation. Early game, Vegas is elite. Long game, the desert asks one question — water — and the fountains only buy you so much time. High ceiling, hard floor, very Vegas.

4. Branson — Survivability Quotient: 6.9

Ozark terrain, winding approach roads, a population with practical skills, and dozens of theaters that are basically windowless concrete arks with commercial kitchens. Branson also has a fleet of duck boats, which means amphibious escape onto Table Rock Lake. Nobody is following you onto the lake. The lake is yours.

3. Williamsburg — Survivability Quotient: 7.6

The sleeper pick. Williamsburg contains an entire district of people who professionally practice pre-industrial survival: blacksmiths, coopers, candle makers, farmers, and reenactors with working muskets. When the grid goes, everyone else is learning; these folks are simply continuing their shift. There's also a literal defensive fort one town over. The guage by which I measure preparedness is 'who changes least,' and Williamsburg barely changes at all.

2. Pigeon Forge — Survivability Quotient: 8.4

Foothills chokepoints, a legendary concentration of cabin housing with wood stoves and well water, and — critically — moonshine distilleries, which produce fuel, antiseptic, and the region's most stable trade currency in one facility. The parkway is a defensible corridor with mountains on both flanks. Docked one point for the number of go-kart tracks, which serve no survival function but will absolutely be used anyway.

1. Gatlinburg — Survivability Quotient: 9.3

One main road in, mountains on three sides, a national park as your backyard pantry, trout in the streams, black bears as an unpaid perimeter deterrent, and a ski lift that converts to high-ground evacuation. Gatlinburg is a natural fortress that happens to sell fudge. Close the Spur, hold the bypass, and the town is a walled kingdom with pancakes. It was never close, my brother-in-law owes me a steak dinner.

One road in. Mountains on three sides. Fudge.

What Actually Makes a Town Defensible?

Chokepoints beat walls, elevation beats both, and reliable water beats everything — a town with one road in and a river through it starts the scenario at a massive advantage. The full scoreboard:

TownDefensibilityFood ReservesEscape RoutesSQ
Gatlinburg9.58.08.59.3
Pigeon Forge8.58.57.58.4
Williamsburg7.08.07.07.6
Branson7.57.08.06.9
Las Vegas8.09.03.05.5
Orlando2.09.56.02.1
Pro Tip: Every town on this list is a top-tier vacation destination during ordinary, non-apocalyptic time, which is now. Gatlinburg and Branson preview packages both start around $49 for multi-night stays. Scout your fortress in person. Call it reconnaissance. Expense the fudge.

The Real Takeaway

Obviously — legally, spiritually — this is entertainment, and no zombies were consulted. But the argument itself is the best road trip game ever invented, and there's something clarifying about looking at a vacation town and asking what it's really made of. The answer, in every case on this list, is: buffets, kind people, and more infrastructure than you'd guess. Browse all our destinations, pick your fortress, and go argue about it at a pool. That's what the pool is for.

faqs.question: Which US vacation town is the most defensible in a disaster scenario?
faqs.answer: In this tongue-in-cheek ranking, Gatlinburg wins on geography: one main road in, mountains on three sides, a national park food source, and high-ground escape routes. It's satire, but the geography is real.

faqs.question: Is Gatlinburg really only accessible by one main road?
faqs.answer: Essentially yes — the Spur from Pigeon Forge is the primary route, with the Gatlinburg Bypass and a mountain road as alternatives. That constrained access is a real feature of the town's geography.

faqs.question: How much do Gatlinburg vacation packages cost?
faqs.answer: Gatlinburg timeshare preview packages start around $49 for multi-night resort stays, in exchange for attending a 90-120 minute presentation during the trip.

faqs.question: What is there to do in Williamsburg besides the colonial district?
faqs.answer: Williamsburg pairs its living-history district with theme parks, a water park, golf, and outlet shopping, making it one of the most well-rounded family destinations on the East Coast.

faqs.question: Why does Branson have duck boats?
faqs.answer: Branson sits beside Table Rock Lake and Lake Taneycomo, and amphibious tour vehicles have long been part of its tourism scene, alongside dozens of live show theaters.

faqs.question: Which vacation destination has the best food value?
faqs.answer: Cancun all-inclusive packages win on pure food value since meals and drinks are bundled — preview rates run $149-479. Among US towns, Branson and Pigeon Forge buffets deliver the most calories per dollar.

faqs.question: Are these zombie survivability rankings serious?
faqs.answer: No — it's a humor piece built on real geography and real travel details. Every town listed is ranked far more rigorously elsewhere on the site by things like deal prices and resort quality.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest mountain vacation town to visit?
faqs.answer: Gatlinburg and Pigeon Forge are consistently the cheapest mountain destinations via preview packages, with multi-night resort stays from about $49.

title: 13 Waterslides Ranked by the Sound You'll Make Going Down
metaTitle: 13 Waterslides Ranked by the Sound You'll Make | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: Every type of resort waterslide ranked by the involuntary sound it extracts from adults, measured on the Scream Index. From 'wheee' to a noise with no vowels.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: Thirteen types of resort waterslide, ranked by the involuntary sound each one extracts from a grown adult, scored on the Scream Index: vertical drop × surprise factor ÷ dignity retained. The trapdoor slide produced a noise from me with no vowels in it, and a lifeguard described it as noticable from the parking lot.
heroImageAlt: Looping colorful waterslides against a blue sky at a large resort water park
content:
The lifeguard at the top of the trapdoor slide asked if I was ready. I said yes. The floor disappeared. The sound I made on the way down had no vowels in it, and when I surfaced, a child in the splash pool looked at me with genuine clinical concern. This article is dedicated to that child. I'm fine now. Mostly.

Bottom Line Up Front: All thirteen waterslide types, ranked by the sound they extract from adults, using the Scream Index: vertical drop × surprise factor ÷ dignity retained. Resort water parks in Orlando and Cancun carry most of these, often included free with your stay.

Why Rank Slides by Sound Instead of Speed?

Because sound is the honest metric: speed can be measured, but the noise you make is the truth your body tells whether you agree or not. Speed stats are marketing, the sound is testimony. I collected this data personally over many trips, and my wife collected the audio evidence, which she has threatened to release if I ever claim to be a brave person at a dinner party again. Here's the full thirteen, quietest to loudest.

The Rankings, Quietest to Loudest

13. The Kiddie Slide You Rode 'For the Bit' — Sound: a self-concious 'wheee'

Four feet tall. You did it for the family photo. The 'wheee' is performed, not felt, and everyone knows it, the six year olds most of all.

12. The Lazy River Entry Ramp — Sound: contented sigh

Technically a slide by the most generous possible definition. You slide eight inches into a tube. The sigh is real and it's earned.

11. The Broad Family Raft Slide — Sound: communal 'wooOOoo'

Four people in a giant round raft, rotating slowly. The 'woo' travels around the raft like a stadium wave as each person swings to the front of the drop. Genuinely lovely. A bonding sound.

10. The Mat Racer — Sound: competitive grunting

Nobody screams on a mat racer becuase everyone is too busy trying to WIN. Eight lanes of adults face-down on foam mats making the sounds of a regional wrestling meet. I have never once heard joy on a mat racer, only strategy.

9. The Slow Dark Tube That's Darker Than Expected — Sound: 'ha ha... hello?'

Enclosed, gentle, but someone skimped on the translucent sections. The sound is a laugh that decays into a genuine question. You emerge having renegotiated your existance slightly.

8. The Toilet Bowl Funnel — Sound: escalating 'ohhh no no no'

You orbit. That's the thing nobody warns you about — the ORBITING. Each revolution the 'no' gains sincerity, and then the drain takes you and the audio cuts out entirely like a dropped call.

7. The Aging Fiberglass Classic at a Certain Older Resort — Sound: 'oof' (percussive, rhythmic)

Every panel seam since 1987 announces itself to your spine individually. It's not a scream, it's percussion, you are the instrument. I rode one of these at a certain resort that shall remain anonymous and my back filed a formal compliant.

6. The See-Through Loop Section — Sound: a whispered swear

The clear section exists so your family can watch your face, and your face knows it. The sound is quiet but the security camera footage is forever.

5. The Aqua Coaster That Goes UP — Sound: confused 'whoa-WHOA?'

Water jets push you uphill, violating everything you believed about slides. The first 'whoa' is delight, the second is your inner physicist resigning on the spot.

4. The Speed Slide From Genuinely Too High — Sound: sustained vowel, pitch rising

A single open flume from a tower you can see from the highway. One long 'aaaAAAA' that Doppler-shifts for observers below. Should of stretched first. Dignity retained: 15 percent.

3. The Drop Slide Into Open Air — Sound: bark, then silence, then splash

The slide ends and for a moment you are simply an item in the sky. The bark is involuntary, the silence is freefall, the splash is embarassing in direct proportion to your entry angle.

2. The Racing Slide Against Your Own Child — Sound: fake laughing that becomes real screaming

It starts as parenting and ends as survival. You will hear yourself make both sounds within three seconds and you're child will win, they always win, they weigh nothing and fear nothing.

1. The Trapdoor Launch Capsule — Sound: [no vowels]

You stand in a clear capsule. A countdown happens TO you. The floor leaves. The resulting sound is consonants only — a 'khhkt' or a 'pfhh' — because vowels require air and the air is still up in the capsule. Highest possible Scream Index: max drop, max surprise, dignity retained: zero, and I'd do it again this weekend.

The floor leaves. The vowels stay behind.

Where Can You Ride All Thirteen Without Buying Water Park Tickets?

Resort water parks are the budget cheat code: many big resorts include full slide complexes free for guests, and preview-rate packages get you those same guest privileges from $49-149. Here's how the access math works:

OptionTypical CostSlide VarietyValue Verdict
Standalone water park day tickets (family of 4)$200-320/dayMaximumFun, pricey
Resort with slide complex, rack rate$250-400/nightHighIncluded, but the room costs plenty
Same resort via preview package$49-149 totalHighThe cheat code
Cancun all-inclusive with slides$149-479 totalHigh + swim-up barElite
Pro Tip: Ride the scariest slide FIRST, before your brain collects opinions about it. Lines are shortest in the first hour, your nerve is freshest, and the rest of the day feels like a victory lap. Also: one hand on the swimsuit waistband on any slide ranked 4 or higher. Veterans know.

The Sound Is the Souvenir

Photos fade, but your family will re-enact the noise you made on the trapdoor slide at every holiday gathering for the rest of natural time. That's the real economy of a water park: you're purchasing stories at volume. Slide-heavy resorts across Cancun and Orlando are all over our current deals, most in the double digits. Go make a sound with no vowels in it.

faqs.question: Do resort water parks cost extra for guests?
faqs.answer: At most resorts with on-site water parks, slides and pools are included free for registered guests. A few larger complexes charge a small daily amenity fee, so check before booking.

faqs.question: What is a trapdoor waterslide?
faqs.answer: It's a slide where you stand in an enclosed capsule and the floor drops away, launching you into a near-vertical descent. It delivers the highest acceleration of any common slide type.

faqs.question: What is the scariest type of waterslide?
faqs.answer: Most riders rank trapdoor launch capsules first, followed by open drop slides and high speed slides. Funnel and bowl slides feel wilder than they are; enclosed dark tubes are gentler than they look.

faqs.question: Are resort waterslides safe for kids?
faqs.answer: Yes, within posted height and age limits, which are enforced by lifeguards at every major resort. Family raft slides and mat racers are designed for mixed ages riding together.

faqs.question: How can a family ride waterslides cheaply in Orlando?
faqs.answer: Book a resort that has its own slide complex through a preview package — commonly $49-99 for multi-night stays — and slide access is included as a guest amenity, avoiding $50-80 per person day tickets.

faqs.question: When are water park lines shortest at resorts?
faqs.answer: The first hour after opening and the late afternoon dinner lull have the shortest lines. Midday between 11am and 3pm is peak wait time.

faqs.question: Why do you have to cross your arms on some waterslides?
faqs.answer: Crossed arms and legs keep your body narrow and streamlined, preventing elbow and heel contact with the flume on fast slides. It's a safety requirement, not a suggestion.

faqs.question: Do Cancun all-inclusive resorts have waterslides?
faqs.answer: Many do — several Cancun and Riviera Maya all-inclusives feature full slide towers and kids' aqua parks, all included in the all-inclusive rate along with meals and drinks.

title: The Cannonball Index: Rating Resort Pools by Splash Potential
metaTitle: The Cannonball Index: Pools Rated by Splash | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: Eight resort pool types rated by cannonball splash potential using the Cannonball Index: depth, runway, and lifeguard leniency versus sunbather proximity.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: The Cannonball Index rates any pool by splash potential: depth × runway length × lifeguard leniency ÷ sunbather proximity. I applied it to all eight resort pool types with help from my nephew, a true amatuer scientist, and the results will change how you see every pool deck forever.
heroImageAlt: Mid-air cannonball over a bright blue resort pool with a huge splash forming
content:
My nephew is eleven and built like a bowling ball with opinions. Last summer, at a pool I won't name, he produced a splash that reached a woman reading a paperback four loungers deep. She lowered the book, looked at him, and slowly began to applaud. That was the day the Cannonball Index was born, we had to make it official.

Bottom Line Up Front: The Cannonball Index rates pools on splash potential: depth × runway length × lifeguard leniency ÷ sunbather proximity. Deep quiet corners of big family pools score highest, adult-only infinity pools are a career-ending mistake, and the best cannonball pools in America are attached to resorts you can book from $49 on our deals page.

What Makes a Pool Good for Cannonballs?

Four factors decide everything: water depth (five feet minimum for full tuck deployment), runway length (three unobstructed strides), lifeguard leniency (posted rules and enforcement enthusiasm), and sunbather proximity (the ethical variable). Splash physics rewards mass moving fast into deep water, but pool ECOSYSTEMS reward reading the room. A perfect cannonball into the wrong pool is not a triumph, it's an incident, and me and my nephew have generated both kinds of data.

The Index, Pool by Pool

1. The Deep End of the Big Family Pool — Index: 9.4

The promised land. Six feet of water, festival atmosphere, lifeguards who's whole vibe is 'keep it reasonable,' and an audience that WANTS you to succeed. Splash lands on people who are already wet. This is where records are set and applause occassionally happens.

2. The Waterslide Splash Pool (During Off-Hours) — Index: 8.6

Engineered for impact absorption — deep, wide, drains built for turbulence. When the slide closes for the evening but the pool stays open, you have a purpose-built splash laboratory. Respect the ropes when the slide is running, obviously. We're scientists, not menaces.

3. The Grotto Pool Behind the Waterfall — Index: 7.2

Solid depth and the waterfall provides sound cover, so a cannonball here is a private performance. Points off for low rock ceilings — check you're clearance, one enthusiastic leap in a grotto teaches a lesson you keep forever.

4. The Rooftop Pool in Vegas — Index: 5.8

Depth is usually humiliating (3'6" of pure marketing) but the leniency in Vegas is legendary and the skyline backdrop adds style points no other venue can offer. A shallow-water flop performed confidently at a Vegas rooftop reads as intentional. That's worth something.

5. The Lazy River — Index: 4.0

Tempting, wrong. Three feet deep, full of drifting tubers who did not consent to participate in your research. The current also carries the evidence — and the splashed parties — directly past you on a loop, so their's no escaping the consequences. One demonstration lap was enough for us.

6. The Kiddie Splash Pad — Index: 2.5, Ethics: 0.0

Eighteen inches of water. Structurally worthless for splash generation and morally indefensible. The Index does not merely score poorly here, the Index looks away.

7. The Adults-Only Quiet Pool — Index: 1.1

Deep enough, technically. But this is a library with water in it, forty people are marinating in silence with cucumber water, and a cannonball here doesn't make you a legend, it makes you a story they tell at other pools. Total leniency score: negative. Do not.

8. The Infinity Edge Pool — Index: 0.3

The splash goes OVER THE EDGE. Your masterpiece literally leaves the venue, wasted on a scenic gutter system, while every person at the swim-up bar recieves your wake. Highest style, lowest return, angriest audience. The Index respects the edge and stays off it.

She lowered her paperback. And she applauded.

How Do You Actually Maximize a Splash?

Maximum splash comes from the tightest possible tuck at the moment of impact — knees to chest, chin down, arms locked around shins — because splash height scales with how much water you displace how suddenly. The full technique matrix:

TechniqueSplash HeightDifficultyNotes
Classic tuckHighEasyThe reliable standard
Late tuck ('the depth charge')Very highMediumTuck at the last instant; timing is everything
Can opener (one leg extended)Very high, directionalMediumAimable. Use responsibly.
Belly flopWide, not tallFreeCosts skin. Earns legend status.
Pro Tip: Always check posted pool depth and rules first — plenty of resort pools prohibit diving and jumping in shallow zones, and lifeguards enforce it. The deep end of the main family pool during mid-afternoon peak chaos is both the safest and most appreciative venue for the art form.

Where the Great Cannonball Pools Live

The best cannonball venues are big family resorts with multi-pool complexes, because multiple pools mean one of them is always in a permissive mood. Orlando leads the world in six-foot deep ends per square mile, and villa resorts bookable on 3-night packages give you enough days to conduct a full research program. Pack a towel with absorbency ratings you trust. Bring an eleven year old if you can, they understand things about physics we've forgotten.

One last note on scoring etiquette: the Index is self-reported, honor-system science, and it must stay that way. You do not rate your own cannonball above an 8 in public — the audience assigns greatness, you merely displace the water. My nephew learned this the day he awarded himself a 10 and the paperback woman quietly revised it to a 9.5 on splash-radius grounds. He accepted the ruling. Scientists respect peer review.

The pools are out there. The Index is yours now. Browse the current deals and go displace some water.

faqs.question: How deep should a pool be for cannonballs?
faqs.answer: Five feet is a sensible minimum for adults doing tucked jumps, and six feet is better. Always check posted depths and pool rules — many resort pools restrict jumping to designated areas.

faqs.question: Are cannonballs allowed at resort pools?
faqs.answer: It depends on the pool. Family pools with deep ends usually allow jumping where posted; adults-only pools, shallow pools, and swim-up bar areas typically prohibit it. Lifeguard instructions always win.

faqs.question: What makes the biggest splash in a pool?
faqs.answer: A tight tuck at the moment of impact displaces the most water most suddenly, which maximizes splash height. Body mass and entry speed help, which is why kids' technique matters more than size.

faqs.question: Why do infinity pools have an edge you can see over?
faqs.answer: The vanishing edge spills water into a hidden catch basin below, which is recirculated. It's engineered scenery — and it means splashes near the edge literally leave the pool.

faqs.question: Do resorts have multiple pools?
faqs.answer: Most large family resorts have several — a main family pool, quiet adult pool, kids' splash areas, and sometimes slide complexes. Multi-pool resorts are common in Orlando and on preview packages.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest way to stay at a resort with a big pool complex?
faqs.answer: Timeshare preview packages regularly put families in multi-pool resorts for $49-149 total for several nights, in exchange for attending a 90-120 minute presentation.

faqs.question: Is a belly flop dangerous?
faqs.answer: From pool-deck heights a belly flop stings but is rarely harmful for adults; from any real height it can genuinely injure you. Keep flops low, and never dive or flop in shallow water.

faqs.question: When is the best time to have a resort pool to yourself?
faqs.answer: Early morning right at opening and the dinner hour (5:30-7pm) are the emptiest windows at most resorts. Midday is peak occupancy.

title: Every Mini-Golf Course on the Gatlinburg Strip, Ranked by a Sore Loser
metaTitle: Gatlinburg Mini-Golf, Ranked by a Sore Loser | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: A sore loser ranks the Gatlinburg-area mini-golf scene using the Sore Loser Adjusted Score. His wife is 11-2 lifetime. The rankings reflect this.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: My wife is 11-2 against me lifetime in Gatlinburg-area mini-golf, so these rankings use the Sore Loser Adjusted Score: course charm − (strokes lost to spouse × excuse plausibility). The mountain-side courses are definately the best in America and I have lost on every single one of them.
heroImageAlt: Colorful mini-golf course on a hillside in Gatlinburg with mountain scenery behind it
content:
On hole 14 of a mountainside course in Gatlinburg, my ball took the loop, cleared the ramp, rolled back down the ramp, touched my shoe, and stopped. My wife, already on the green in one, wrote something on the scorecard without speaking. We have been married a long time. I know what she wrote. She drew a little face.

Bottom Line Up Front: The Gatlinburg strip and its Pigeon Forge overflow form the densest, best mini-golf corridor in America, and I've lost on all of it — my wife is 11-2 lifetime. Each course below gets a Sore Loser Adjusted Score: course charm − (strokes lost to spouse × excuse plausibility). A $49 Gatlinburg package puts you five minutes from every hole that has ever wronged me.

Why Is Gatlinburg the Mini-Golf Capital of America?

Because the terrain does half the design work: build a course on a Smoky Mountain hillside and gravity becomes a co-designer, giving you multi-level holes that flatland courses could never dream of. Add in sixty years of tourist-town one-upmanship — every course adding one more waterfall, one more animatronic, one more hole inside a fake mountain — and you get a strip where mini-golf evolved like finches on seperate islands. I am ranking the species anonymously here, becuase these are personal grudges, not reviews.

The Rankings (Adjusted for My Suffering)

1. The Hillside One Where the Course Rides Up an Incline Lift — Adjusted Score: 9.1

You ride TO the first hole. It's basically a ski lift for putters and it rules. The downhill routing means every hole breaks toward the valley, which my wife figured out on hole 2 and I figured out in the parking lot afterward. Lost by 6. Still the best course experience in the South, the view from the top tee is worth the price of the whole trip.

2. The One With the Waterfall You Putt Behind — Adjusted Score: 8.4

An actual curtain of water between you and the cup on the signature hole. Charming beyond argument. My ball entered the water feature and I want to say for the record that the drainage grate placement is a choice someone made on purpose. Lost by 4, blamed humidity, excuse plausibility rated 'low' by the scorekeeper.

3. The Indoor Blacklight One That Smells Like 1996 — Adjusted Score: 7.8

Neon planets, glowing carpet, air conditioning that hits like a religious experience after a July afternoon. The blacklight hides ball lines, which should of leveled the playing field. It did not. Lost by 3. The carpet has opinions and their all bad, but the AC earns back every point.

4. The Hilbilly-Themed One With the Outhouse Hole — Adjusted Score: 7.3

Yes, you putt through an outhouse. Yes, there's a moonshine still that gurgles. The theming commits SO hard that losing here almost doesn't sting. Almost. Lost by 2 — my closest match of the era — and I maintain the moonshine still gurgled during my backswing. Twice.

5. The Two-Course Mega Complex Where You Must Choose — Adjusted Score: 6.9
